title: Critical surface strain regime for stable and active epitaxial platinum oxygen reduction electrocatalysts - Nature Energy
gist: Ultrathin epitaxial platinum films on iridium, kept below a thickness-dependent critical surface strain, deliver both high oxygen-reduction activity and durability for PEM fuel cells—1.5 A/mgPt at 0.9V with under 10% loss after 30,000 cycles, far outperforming conventional Pt and PtNi catalysts.

insights.claim: For epitaxial platinum ORR catalysts there exists a thickness-dependent critical surface strain below which irreversible strain relaxation is avoided, defining a thickness window that simultaneously optimizes both catalyst stability and activity.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Provides a tunable design rule for balancing the long-standing activity-vs-durability tradeoff in fuel-cell catalysts.
insights.quote: we demonstrate thickness-dependent critical strain below which irreversible strain relaxation is avoided

insights.claim: First-principles calculations place the critical strain window from -8.2% (compressive) to 2.7% (tensile), with the optimal -2.5% strain maintained in platinum films up to roughly 3 nm thick.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Quantifies the manufacturing target thickness/strain for producing durable high-activity Pt catalysts.
insights.quote: the critical strain values range from −8.2% (compressive) to 2.7% (tensile) and the optimal strain (−2.5%) is maintained in Pt films up to ~3-nm thick

insights.claim: In H2–air PEM fuel cells, platinum films on iridium delivered a mass activity of 1.5 ± 0.3 A/mgPt at 0.9V with less than 10% performance loss after 30,000 cycles, versus under 0.4 A/mgPt and over 60% loss for conventional Pt and PtNi catalysts.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: A roughly 4x activity gain with dramatically better durability could lower Pt loading and cost in fuel-cell vehicles/heavy-duty transport.
insights.quote: Pt films on iridium deliver a mass activity of 1.5 ± 0.3 A mgPt−1 at 0.9 V and less than 10% performance loss after 30,000 cycles

insights.claim: The Pt/Ir ultrathin-film catalyst technology is protected by US Patent US11990626B2 assigned to 3M Company, with 3M employees among the authors and the work funded by the US Department of Energy's hydrogen and fuel-cell programs.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: 3M holds IP on a commercially relevant catalyst advance—relevant for fuel-cell supply-chain and licensing exposure.

insights.claim: Thicker epitaxial films improve stability against platinum dissolution but risk irreversible strain relaxation that reduces catalytic activity, which is why identifying the critical-strain thickness window is necessary to get both properties at once.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Explains the mechanism behind prior catalyst durability failures and how to engineer around it.
insights.quote: Although thick films can improve stability against dissolution, they may suffer from irreversible strain relaxation, reducing catalytic activity.
